metadata.career_relevance: OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities cause 90% of security breaches. Zerodha's trading API had a mass assignment vulnerability — attackers could set their own account balance by adding extra fields to a JSON request. After this topic, you can identify and fix the most common web security vulnerabilities in a Spring Boot application.

hook.what_you_will_build: Bean validation with @Valid, SQL injection prevention with JPA, XSS protection with CSP headers, and a custom validator.

visual_aid.description: OWASP Top 10 attack/defense grid. Left: attack type (SQL injection, XSS, mass assignment, path traversal). Middle: vulnerable code snippet (red). Right: secure code snippet (green). Bottom: security header panel showing CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options all set. Arrow from @Valid annotation → validation failure → 400 Bad Request with field errors JSON.
visual_aid.alt_text: OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities with Java/Spring Boot fixes

worked_example.scenario: Secure Razorpay payment request: validation, mass assignment prevention, SQL injection protection.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. PaymentRequest DTO with @Valid constraints
2. Custom @ValidMerchantId validator
3. Exception handler returning structured 400 errors
4. @SpringBootTest: invalid input → 400, valid → 200

step_by_step_solution.details: SQL injection: use JPA with @Param (always parameterized). Never concatenate user input into queries. Spring Data automatically uses PreparedStatement — safe by default.

Never bind HTTP request directly to a JPA Entity. Use a DTO (Data Transfer Object) with only the fields users are allowed to set. Entity fields like `balance`, `role`, `adminFlag` never appear in the DTO.

worked_example.final_code:
// @Valid on @RequestBody: validates all constraints before controller executes
// MethodArgumentNotValidException → @ExceptionHandler → 400 with field errors
things_to_notice: @Valid vs @Validated: @Valid triggers Bean Validation. @Validated (Spring-specific) adds group validation support. Use @Valid on controller @RequestBody; @Validated on service methods for method-level validation.

Mass assignment: if your DTO contains a field named the same as a sensitive Entity field, an attacker can set it via JSON. Solution: DTOs NEVER contain sensitive fields. If the field name exists in the DTO, it can be set by the client.

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting): if you store user input (description, comments) and display it in HTML, attackers can inject <script> tags. Prevention: (1) sanitize input with OWASP Java HTML Sanitizer, (2) set Content-Security-Policy header, (3) escape output (Thymeleaf does this by default).

Content Security Policy: `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'` tells browsers to only execute scripts from your own domain. Even if an XSS payload is injected, the browser blocks it.

industry_examples.use_case: @Valid on OrderRequest DTO: symbol must match NSE/BSE ticker pattern, quantity max 10000 (circuit breaker), orderType in {BUY, SELL, LIMIT, SL}. Custom @ValidTicker validator: checks against cached list of valid NSE symbols. Catches: typos, delisted symbols, invalid format. Before fix: invalid orders reached the exchange API, causing costly rejections.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you prevent SQL injection in Spring Boot?' → JPA with @Param annotations — all queries use PreparedStatement with parameter binding, never string concatenation. If using native queries: Spring Data @Query with :param bindings. Never: String query = 'SELECT ... WHERE id=' + userInput. Also: input validation with @Valid/@NotNull ensures only expected data reaches the database layer.
applications.real_world_workflow: Security validation: (1) validate input at API boundary (@Valid), (2) use parameterized queries (JPA default), (3) escape output (Thymeleaf), (4) set security headers (Spring Security), (5) use DTOs (not entities) for mass assignment prevention.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using the JPA Entity directly as @RequestBody instead of a DTO. Any field in the entity can be set by the client — including id, createdAt, balance, adminRole.
common_gaps.remediation: Always use a separate DTO (record or class) for incoming requests. Entity: only modified by your business logic. DTO: only the fields the client is explicitly allowed to set. MapStruct or manual mapping: DTO → Entity.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: @PostMapping uses @RequestBody PaymentEntity (not PaymentRequest). Client can set id, version, balance fields via JSON body.

key_takeaways: @Valid: validates constraints before controller executes; failure → 400
DTOs prevent mass assignment: client can only set fields in the DTO
JPA always uses PreparedStatement: SQL injection prevention by default
Never concatenate user input into SQL strings
@ControllerAdvice: global exception handler for 400 field error responses
OWASP A01 Broken Access Control: most common — fixed by JWT + @PreAuthorize

self_check_questions.question: Why is it dangerous to use a JPA Entity directly as a @RequestBody parameter?
self_check_questions.answer: Mass assignment vulnerability. The JPA Entity contains all database fields: id, version, createdAt, balance, adminFlag, etc. When Jackson maps the incoming JSON to the Entity, it sets ANY field present in the JSON, including sensitive ones the client should never control. Example: attacker sends {symbol: RELIANCE, accountBalance: 9999999}. Jackson sets entity.accountBalance = 9999999 → EntityManager persists it → attacker updated their own balance. DTOs fix this: the DTO only has fields the client is ALLOWED to set (symbol, quantity, orderType). accountBalance is not in the DTO → Jackson ignores it in the JSON. Entity fields are only set by your trusted business logic.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 23 topic: Rate Limiting & API Security — protecting APIs from abuse, DDoS, and brute force attacks using Bucket4j, API key authentication, and request throttling.
